passages.explanation: Dorian's entrance at the piano, playing Schumann, establishes him as a creature of instinct and sensation rather than intellect. His petulance about sitting for the portrait and his immediate blush upon seeing Lord Henry reveal a malleability that both men will exploit. Basil's attempt to manage the introduction is already a losing battle -- Dorian is drawn to novelty, and Lord Henry represents everything Basil is not: worldly, provocative, and dangerous.

Lord Henry's first real look at Dorian catalogs his beauty with the precision of an art collector appraising a masterpiece. The description emphasizes Dorian's 'candour of youth' and 'passionate purity' -- qualities that exist precisely because they have not yet been tested. Henry's remark that Dorian is 'too charming to go in for philanthropy' is his first move in a seduction of ideas: redirecting Dorian away from moral engagement and toward self-cultivation.

The power dynamics crystallize here: Basil wants Lord Henry to leave, Dorian insists he stay, and Dorian's wish overrules the artist's. This triangulation -- where the beautiful young man holds the real power without knowing it -- mirrors the way beauty operates throughout the novel as an unconscious tyranny. Dorian's comparison of himself to 'a young Greek martyr' on the painting platform is both playful and prophetic: he is being sacrificed, though the altar is aesthetic rather than religious.

Lord Henry's speech on influence is the intellectual heart of the novel and deeply paradoxical -- he argues against influence while exerting it. His vision of a 'New Hedonism' where one lives fully without repression is seductive philosophy, but it is also a trap: by telling Dorian that self-denial is mutilation, he is planting the seeds of the moral recklessness that will destroy him. The claim that 'the only way to get rid of a temptation is to yield to it' sounds like liberation but is really a recipe for enslavement to desire.

Dorian's stunned silence after Lord Henry's speech marks the precise moment of his corruption -- or his awakening, depending on your perspective. Wilde's description of 'entirely fresh influences at work within him' that 'seemed to have come really from himself' captures the insidious nature of ideological seduction: the best manipulation feels like self-discovery. The meditation on words as more powerful and 'real' than music speaks to Wilde's belief in language as the most dangerous of all art forms.

The garden scene is an Eden moment, complete with sensory temptation. Lord Henry's aphorism that 'nothing can cure the soul but the senses, just as nothing can cure the senses but the soul' sounds balanced but actually privileges sensation over reflection. Dorian's conflicted response -- attracted to Henry yet 'afraid of him, and ashamed of being afraid' -- captures the psychological complexity of encountering someone who seems to understand you better than you understand yourself. The fact that Henry has revealed Dorian to himself in minutes, while Basil's months of friendship 'never altered him,' shows the superior power of intellectual seduction over emotional devotion.

Lord Henry's long speech on youth is the novel's most famous set piece, and it works as both genuine philosophy and calculated manipulation. By making Dorian terrified of aging, Henry creates the psychic wound that the entire plot will flow from. The speech's beauty is part of its danger -- it is so eloquently expressed that its nihilism feels like wisdom. Dorian's response, watching a bee with 'that strange interest in trivial things that we try to develop when things of high import make us afraid,' is one of Wilde's most psychologically acute observations about how we distract ourselves from transformative moments.

The exchange about 'always' being glad is a miniature of the novel's larger argument: Dorian wants permanence, Henry insists everything is fleeting. Dorian's bold suggestion that their friendship should be 'a caprice' shows how quickly he has absorbed Henry's philosophy -- he is already performing the role of the aesthete, though he flushes at his own boldness, revealing the innocent beneath the new pose.

The completion of the portrait triggers Dorian's existential crisis. Seeing his own beauty externalized makes him conscious of it for the first time, and that consciousness immediately becomes anguish because beauty now has a visible standard against which to measure its inevitable decline. This is the Narcissus myth reimagined: the reflection does not just captivate but torments, because time will destroy the original while the image endures. Wilde is dramatizing the uniquely modern agony of self-awareness -- the curse of knowing what you are and what you will lose.

Dorian's wish -- 'If it were I who was to be always young, and the picture that was to grow old! For that I would give my soul' -- is the Faustian bargain that drives the entire novel. What makes it devastating is that it springs not from evil but from the collision of innocence with a new awareness of mortality. His sudden cruelty toward Basil ('I am no more to you than a green bronze figure') shows how quickly the aestheticist worldview corrupts: if people are just beautiful objects, then they can accuse others of the same reduction. The jealousy Dorian feels toward his own portrait is the birth of his duality.

The aftermath splits the three characters into their essential roles: Basil blames Henry, Henry deflects with wit, and Dorian oscillates between anguish and possessiveness. Basil's impulse to destroy the painting -- and Dorian's cry that it 'would be murder' -- prefigures the novel's climax and establishes the portrait as a living thing, a second self. The final exchange where Dorian refuses to dine with Basil in favor of going to the theatre with Henry dramatizes the decisive shift in allegiance: Dorian has chosen pleasure over devotion, the wit over the artist.

Lord Henry's remarks on sin as 'the only real colour-element left in modern life' and Basil's plaintive decision to 'stay with the real Dorian' while the flesh-and-blood Dorian leaves with Henry crystallize the novel's central question: which is the real Dorian -- the painted image or the living person? Basil's final pain as the door closes signals his awareness that he has already lost Dorian to Henry's influence. The chapter ends with a subtle foreshadowing: fidelity, appearance, and reality are already being destabilized, and the two Dorians -- portrait and person -- have begun to diverge.
